title: Research methods in psychology
summary: Psychology is an evidence-based discipline, and the method a researcher chooses decides what conclusions the evidence can support. This lesson builds the core research methods — the experimental method (IV/DV, control, lab/field/natural experiments and cause-effect), correlational studies, case studies and observations — alongside qualitative versus quantitative data, sampling and generalisability, and the twin ideas of validity and reliability. The unifying question throughout is: given this method, what can we honestly claim, and what can we not?
learningObjectives: Define and distinguish the main research methods — the experimental method, correlational studies, case studies and observations — and identify each from a described study.
Explain how independent and dependent variables, control of variables and experimental type (lab, field, natural) allow an experiment to establish cause and effect.
Distinguish qualitative from quantitative data and evaluate sampling techniques (random, opportunity, self-selected, purposive) in terms of generalisability.
Apply the concepts of validity (internal, external, ecological) and reliability to judge how a method choice limits the conclusions that can be drawn.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: The Method Decides the Claim
simpleExplanation.summary: Every research method is a different kind of lens. An experiment can show that one thing CAUSES another; a correlation can only show that two things move TOGETHER; a case study gives rich depth about one case but cannot be generalised widely. Before you evaluate any study, ask what its method actually allows you to conclude.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of methods like tools in a kit. A hammer (the experiment) is powerful for one job — driving the nail of cause and effect — because you control everything except the one variable you change. But you would not use a hammer to measure a room. A tape measure (a correlation) tells you how two things relate in size, but never that one made the other happen. Picking the wrong tool for the claim is the single most common way students and researchers go wrong.
steps: Identify the method from what the researcher actually did — did they manipulate a variable, or just measure two that already existed?
Match the method to the claim it can support: experiments for cause-effect, correlations for relationships only.
Check the sample and setting: who took part, and can the findings generalise beyond them?
Weigh validity and reliability to decide how much trust the conclusion deserves.

flashcards.back: The variable the researcher deliberately manipulates to observe its effect on another variable. It is the presumed 'cause' in an experiment. Must be operationalised (defined in measurable terms).
The variable that is measured to see whether the IV had an effect. It is the presumed 'effect'. Also must be operationalised, e.g. 'memory' becomes 'number of words correctly recalled'.

Holding all other relevant variables constant so that any change in the DV can be attributed to the IV alone. Control is what lets an experiment claim cause and effect; without it, confounding variables offer rival explanations.
An uncontrolled variable, other than the IV, that varies systematically with it and offers an alternative explanation for the results. Confounds threaten internal validity.

flashcards.front: Laboratory vs field vs natural experiment
flashcards.back: LAB: high control in an artificial setting. FIELD: IV manipulated in a real-world setting (more ecological validity, less control). NATURAL: the researcher does not manipulate the IV but studies a naturally occurring one (e.g. a disaster), so causal claims are weaker.

Measures two co-variables to see whether they are related, without manipulating anything. It can show the strength and direction of a relationship but CANNOT establish cause and effect (third-variable and bidirectionality problems).

An in-depth, often longitudinal investigation of a single individual, group or event, usually using multiple sources of data. Rich and holistic, but findings are hard to generalise and may be affected by researcher bias.

NATURALISTIC = watching behaviour in its normal setting; PARTICIPANT = the observer joins the group. OVERT = participants know they are watched (risk of reactivity); COVERT = they do not (better validity but raises consent issues).

QUANTITATIVE = numerical data that can be statistically analysed (reaction times, test scores). QUALITATIVE = non-numerical, descriptive data capturing meaning and experience (interview transcripts, field notes). Method choice usually determines which you get.

RANDOM = every member of the target population has an equal chance (best for representativeness). OPPORTUNITY = whoever is available (convenient but often biased). SELF-SELECTED (volunteer) = people respond to an advert (volunteer bias). PURPOSIVE = deliberately chosen for a characteristic (common in qualitative work).

The extent to which findings from a sample can be applied to the wider target population. A biased or small sample (e.g. opportunity sample of psychology students) limits generalisability, however well the study is run.

VALIDITY = accuracy: does the study measure what it claims to? INTERNAL = free of confounds; EXTERNAL = generalises beyond the study; ECOLOGICAL = generalises to real-life settings. RELIABILITY = consistency: would repeating the study give the same results? A study can be reliable yet not valid.

A correlation between two variables NEVER on its own proves one causes the other. There may be a third variable, or the causation may run the other way. Only a controlled experiment supports a cause-effect claim.

quickCheck.prompt: A study finds that the more hours students sleep, the higher their exam scores. A student writes: 'This shows sleep improves exam performance.' The best evaluation is:
options: Correct — the relationship proves sleep causes higher scores
Flawed — a correlation cannot establish cause and effect; a third variable (e.g. conscientiousness) could explain both
Correct, but only if the sample is large
Flawed — because the DV was not operationalised
quickCheck.answer: Flawed — a correlation cannot establish cause and effect; a third variable (e.g. conscientiousness) could explain both

quickCheck.prompt: Which feature of the experimental method is what allows it — unlike a correlation — to support a cause-effect conclusion?
options: It always uses a large random sample
It collects quantitative rather than qualitative data
The researcher manipulates the IV while controlling other variables
It takes place in a laboratory
quickCheck.answer: The researcher manipulates the IV while controlling other variables

quickCheck.prompt: A researcher advertises for volunteers on a psychology forum and studies the first 30 who reply. The main threat this sampling method poses to the conclusions is:
options: Low reliability, because the study cannot be repeated
Volunteer (self-selection) bias, which limits generalisability to the wider population
A confounding variable in the IV
Lack of a dependent variable
quickCheck.answer: Volunteer (self-selection) bias, which limits generalisability to the wider population

quickCheck.prompt: A memory test gives almost identical scores every time it is re-administered to the same people, but psychologists argue it actually measures general intelligence, not memory. This test is best described as:
options: Valid but not reliable
Reliable but not valid
Both valid and reliable
Neither valid nor reliable

quickCheck.prompt: A tightly controlled lab experiment on memory uses a list of nonsense syllables recalled under strict timing. Its most likely limitation is:
options: Low internal validity, because the IV was not manipulated
Low ecological validity, because the artificial task and setting may not reflect real-life memory
That it establishes only a correlation, not causation
That it collects qualitative rather than quantitative data
quickCheck.answer: Low ecological validity, because the artificial task and setting may not reflect real-life memory

faq.q: What is the single most important difference between an experiment and a correlational study?
faq.a: Manipulation and control. In an experiment the researcher actively changes the independent variable and holds other variables constant, so any change in the dependent variable can be attributed to the IV — this is what licenses a cause-effect claim. In a correlational study nothing is manipulated; two variables that already vary in the world are simply measured and compared. That is why a correlation, no matter how strong, can only describe a relationship and can never on its own prove causation.

faq.q: If lab experiments are 'artificial', why do psychologists still use them?
faq.a: Because control is the price of causal certainty. A laboratory lets the researcher strip out confounding variables and isolate the effect of the IV, giving high internal validity. The trade-off is ecological validity — the artificial task or setting may not reflect real behaviour. Good research often uses several methods: a lab experiment to establish that an effect exists, then a field study to check it holds in real life.

faq.q: How does the choice of method limit the conclusions I can draw?
faq.a: Directly, and this is the heart of the topic. A case study gives rich depth about one person but cannot be generalised to everyone. A correlation shows a relationship but not a cause. A covert naturalistic observation captures genuine behaviour but cannot explain why it occurred. When you evaluate a study, always tie your point back to its method: the method sets a ceiling on what the findings can legitimately be used to claim.

faq.q: Can a study be reliable but not valid?
faq.a: Yes, and the reverse is the classic exam distinction. Reliability is consistency — repeating the study gives the same result. Validity is accuracy — the study measures what it claims to. A bathroom scale that always reads 3 kg too heavy is perfectly reliable (consistent) but invalid (wrong). In psychology, a questionnaire could give stable scores every time yet still fail to measure the construct it claims to, so it would be reliable but not valid.

sections.content: Psychology is an empirical, evidence-based subject, and the value of any conclusion depends entirely on how the evidence was gathered. This lesson is about that link. We will build the main research methods — the experimental method, correlational studies, case studies and observations — and the tools used to judge them: qualitative versus quantitative data, sampling and generalisability, and validity versus reliability. Throughout, keep one question in view: given the method the researcher used, what can we honestly conclude, and what can we not?
The experimental method: manipulation, control and cause-effect

The experimental method is the only method that can establish cause and effect. The researcher manipulates one variable — the independent variable (IV) — and measures its effect on another — the dependent variable (DV) — while controlling all other relevant variables. Both variables must be operationalised: defined in specific, measurable terms. 'Memory' is not measurable, but 'the number of words correctly recalled from a 20-item list in two minutes' is.

Control is what makes the causal claim possible. If everything except the IV is held constant, then any change in the DV must be due to the IV rather than to some other factor. An uncontrolled factor that varies with the IV is a confounding variable, and it offers a rival explanation that undermines the study. The whole design of an experiment is really an effort to rule out these rival explanations.

items: **Independent variable (IV):** what the researcher manipulates — the presumed cause.
**Dependent variable (DV):** what the researcher measures — the presumed effect.
**Control:** holding other variables constant so the IV is the only thing that changes.
**Confounding variable:** an uncontrolled variable that offers an alternative explanation and threatens internal validity.
Only the experimental method, through manipulation and control, supports a **cause-effect** conclusion.

sections.content: Experiments come in three types that trade control against realism. A laboratory experiment offers the highest control in an artificial setting, maximising internal validity but often at the cost of ecological validity. A field experiment manipulates the IV in a real-world setting, gaining ecological validity but sacrificing some control. A natural experiment studies a naturally occurring IV that the researcher cannot manipulate (such as the effect of a disaster or a policy change); because the IV is not truly manipulated, causal claims are weaker and confounds harder to exclude.

sections.question:
Read the following stimulus and identify the method used, then state one strength and one limitation that follow from it.

A psychologist tested whether background noise affects concentration. She recruited 60 office workers and randomly allocated them to two groups. Group A completed a 10-minute proofreading task in a silent room; Group B completed the identical task while office chatter was played at a fixed volume. She recorded the number of errors each participant missed.
sections.solution:
**Method:** This is a laboratory experiment. The researcher manipulated an independent variable (presence versus absence of background noise) and measured a dependent variable (number of errors missed) while controlling the task, the time and the setting. Random allocation to conditions is a further clue that this is a true experiment.

**One strength (follows from the method):** Because the IV was manipulated and other variables were controlled, the study can support a cause-effect conclusion — any difference in errors between the groups can reasonably be attributed to the noise rather than to some other factor. This gives the study high internal validity.

**One limitation (follows from the method):** The controlled, artificial task (timed proofreading in a set room) may not reflect how people concentrate during real, varied office work, so the study has limited ecological validity. The findings may not generalise to genuine workplace behaviour.

sections.content: Correlational studies: relationships, not causation

A correlational study measures two variables that already exist and examines whether they are related — it manipulates nothing. It can tell you the direction (positive or negative) and strength of a relationship, which is valuable when manipulation would be impractical or unethical. But it has a hard limit: a correlation can never, on its own, establish cause and effect. Two rival explanations always remain. First, the bidirectionality problem: even if A and B are related, A might cause B or B might cause A. Second, the third-variable problem: some other factor may cause both. A famous illustration is that ice-cream sales correlate with drowning — but neither causes the other; hot weather (the third variable) drives both.

The phrase examiners want to see is that a correlation shows 'a relationship between two variables, not a cause-effect relationship'. If a study only measured variables (rather than manipulating one), any causal wording — 'X increases Y', 'X leads to Y' — is a mistake. Say instead 'X is associated with Y' or 'X and Y are correlated'.
Case studies and observations

A case study is an in-depth, often longitudinal investigation of a single individual, group or event, usually drawing on several sources of data (interviews, observations, records). Its great strength is depth and holism — it can explore rare or complex cases (such as a patient with unusual brain damage) in a richness no experiment could match. Its limitations follow directly from its method: findings from a single case are difficult to generalise, and the close involvement of the researcher can introduce bias.

Observations record behaviour as it occurs and vary along two dimensions. A naturalistic observation watches behaviour in its normal setting; in a participant observation the researcher joins the group being studied. Independently, observation can be overt (participants know they are being watched, which risks reactivity — people behaving differently because they are observed) or covert (they do not know, which improves validity but raises consent and privacy concerns). As with every method, the choice shapes the conclusions: a covert naturalistic observation captures genuine behaviour but cannot easily explain why it happened.

items: **Case study:** deep, holistic, ideal for rare cases; but hard to generalise and vulnerable to researcher bias.
**Naturalistic observation:** behaviour in its real setting — high ecological validity, low control.
**Participant observation:** the researcher joins the group — rich insight, but risk of losing objectivity.
**Overt vs covert:** overt risks reactivity (demand characteristics); covert reduces it but raises ethical issues around consent.

sections.content: Qualitative vs quantitative data

Data come in two broad kinds, and method choice usually determines which. Quantitative data are numerical and can be statistically analysed — reaction times, error counts, questionnaire scores. They allow objective comparison and are typical of experiments and correlational studies. Qualitative data are non-numerical and descriptive, capturing meaning and experience — interview transcripts, field notes, open-ended responses. They offer depth and context and are typical of case studies and many observations. Neither is superior; they answer different questions. Quantitative data are good for measuring how much or how many; qualitative data are good for understanding why and how something is experienced.
Sampling and generalisability

A sample is the group actually studied; the target population is the wider group the researcher wants to describe. Generalisability is the extent to which findings from the sample can be applied to that population, and it depends heavily on how the sample was drawn. Even a flawlessly conducted study tells you little about the wider world if its sample is biased.

items: **Random sampling:** every member of the target population has an equal chance of selection. Best for representativeness, but demands a full list of the population and is often impractical.
**Opportunity sampling:** using whoever is conveniently available (e.g. students in the researcher's class). Quick and cheap, but usually unrepresentative.
**Self-selected (volunteer) sampling:** participants respond to an advert. Easy to gather, but prone to volunteer bias — volunteers may differ systematically from those who do not come forward.
**Purposive sampling:** deliberately selecting participants with a specific characteristic relevant to the research. Common in qualitative work, but not designed to be statistically representative.

sections.content: Validity and reliability

These two ideas are the criteria by which any study is judged, and confusing them is a classic error. Validity is about accuracy — does the study measure what it claims to measure? Reliability is about consistency — would repeating the study produce the same results? A study can be reliable but not valid: a scale that always reads 3 kg too heavy is perfectly consistent yet always wrong.

items: **Internal validity:** whether the study is free of confounding variables, so the IV really is responsible for the change in the DV. Threatened by poor control, demand characteristics and researcher bias.
**External validity:** whether findings generalise beyond the specifics of the study — to other people, settings and times.
**Ecological validity:** a form of external validity — whether findings generalise to real-life, everyday settings. Often low in tightly controlled lab tasks.
**Reliability:** consistency of results on repetition. High control tends to raise reliability, sometimes at the expense of ecological validity.

sections.content: How method choice shapes the conclusion

Every strand of this lesson points to a single principle: the method sets a ceiling on what a study can legitimately claim. An experiment can claim causation but may lack realism; a correlation can claim a relationship but never a cause; a case study offers depth but not breadth; an observation captures real behaviour but not its reasons. A strong evaluation always ties its point back to the method — not 'the study is weak', but 'because this was a correlational study, it cannot show that X caused Y, only that they are related'.

sections.question:
Read the following stimulus and answer the question below.

A health researcher surveyed 500 adults, measuring how many hours of social media they used per day and their score on a standardised loneliness questionnaire. She found that people who used social media more tended to report higher loneliness scores. A newspaper reported the study under the headline: 'Study proves social media makes people lonely.'

**Question:** Identify the research method used and explain why the newspaper's conclusion is not justified. [4 marks]
sections.solution:
**Method:** This is a correlational study. The researcher did not manipulate any variable; she measured two co-variables that already existed (daily social media use and loneliness score) and examined the relationship between them.

**Why the conclusion is not justified:**
1. A correlational study can only show that two variables are associated, not that one causes the other. The design lacks the manipulation and control of an experiment, so no cause-effect claim is possible.
2. The direction of causation is unclear (bidirectionality): rather than social media causing loneliness, it may be that lonely people turn to social media more.
3. A third variable could explain both — for example, unemployment or poor mental health might independently increase both social media use and loneliness.

Because of these limitations, the most the study supports is that social media use and loneliness are correlated; the headline's causal claim ('makes people lonely') goes beyond what the method allows.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Treating a correlation as proof of cause** — the commonest error. A correlation shows a relationship only; always mention the third-variable or bidirectionality problem when a causal claim is made from correlational data.
**Saying an experiment 'shows a link'** — undersell in the other direction. A controlled experiment can support a genuine cause-effect claim; do not downgrade it to a mere 'association'.
**Confusing validity with reliability** — validity is accuracy (measuring the right thing); reliability is consistency (same result on repetition). A study can be reliable yet not valid.

**Ignoring the sample when judging generalisability** — a well-run study on a biased sample (e.g. an opportunity sample of psychology undergraduates) still cannot be generalised to the wider population.
**Failing to operationalise variables** — writing 'the IV was music' or 'the DV was memory' is too vague; state how each was manipulated or measured.
**Not linking the evaluation to the method** — a strength or limitation only earns credit when it follows from the method used and is explained, not merely named.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real markband

sections.question: Explain one strength and one limitation of using the experimental method in psychological research. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**
One strength of the experimental method is that it can establish cause and effect. Because the researcher manipulates the independent variable while controlling other variables, any change in the dependent variable can be attributed to the IV rather than to a confounding factor — for example, in a memory experiment the researcher can conclude that the type of rehearsal, and not some other variable, caused the difference in recall. This control gives experiments high internal validity.

One limitation is that experiments, especially laboratory experiments, often have low ecological validity. The tightly controlled, artificial task and setting may not reflect how behaviour occurs in real life — for instance, recalling nonsense syllables in a lab tells us little about how people remember everyday events. As a result, the findings may not generalise beyond the study.

---
**How our marking engine awards the marks (4 marks):**
Psychology research-methods questions are marked by our analytic points-based marking: each distinct valid point is worth 1 mark. For this question the engine looks for four creditworthy points:
- **1 mark** — a named, correct strength: that the experiment establishes cause and effect via manipulation and control of the IV.
- **1 mark** — accurate explanation/elaboration of that strength (why control rules out confounds → internal validity). The 'A' (analysis) mark is awarded for a correctly explained point, not merely a named one.
- **1 mark** — a named, correct limitation: low ecological validity / artificial setting.
- **1 mark** — accurate explanation of that limitation (why an artificial task limits generalisation to real life).

The engine applies **error-carried-forward (ECF)**: if you name a valid strength but then explain a slightly different but still-valid mechanism, the explanation mark is still available. It also **accepts equivalents** — any correctly explained strength (e.g. high control, replicability/reliability, standardised procedures) and any correctly explained limitation (e.g. demand characteristics, low mundane realism, sampling issues) earns the marks. What is NOT credited: naming a point without explaining it (that caps you at the 'identify' marks), or a point that does not actually follow from the experimental method.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does: write your own answer to this question, submit it, and our marking engine awards a mark for each distinct valid point — a named strength, its explanation, a named limitation, its explanation — applying ECF and accepting equivalents. The habit that scores: name the point, then EXPLAIN why it follows from the method.

sections.label: Get a Paper 3 research-methods answer marked: explain a strength and a limitation of the experimental method
